answer:
**The requested section is Chapter 2** of Charles Dunoyer's *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, titled **"I. Influence de la race sur la liberté"** ("I. Influence of race on liberty").[^1]

This chapter (85 paragraphs, ~10,000 words) argues that human races exhibit innate physical and intellectual differences that affect their capacity for development, civilization, and thus liberty (defined as the perfected exercise of faculties). Dunoyer draws on physiologists like Blumenbach, Lawrence, and Magendie to classify five main races (Caucasian/white as central and superior; Mongolian/yellow; Ethiopian/black; American/red; Malayan/brown), emphasizing cranial differences, physical vigor, and civilizational progress. He defends the idea against critics like Comte, insisting races are unequally perfectible, with the white race showing unique progressive capacity, while rejecting injustice toward others.

### Key opening:
> [p-27e58b] **CHAP. 2: I. Influence of race on liberty.**  
> § 1. Men, I have said, are all the freer as they have more developed their faculties and better learned to regulate their use. But first, are the faculties of all races of men susceptible of the same degree of rectitude and development?[^2]

### Representative key passage (TextRank #1, on inequality of races as extension of individual inequality, limited mixing, and examples):
> [p-16f1c1] I have some difficulty understanding why there has been such a strong outcry against this idea of the inequality of races. It seems to me, however, that it offers nothing more strange and more paradoxical than that of the inequality of individuals. If one individual can differ from another, one does not see why ten individuals could not differ from ten others, a thousand from a thousand others. [...] The Jewish nation is one example. The Scots are another.[^3]

### Closing note (with Blumenbach classification):
> [p-160bb0] [^20]: This classification, which belongs to Blumenbach (*De gen. hum. variet. nativa*), has been adopted by W. Lawrence (*Lectures on physiology, zoology and the natural history of man*, pp. 549 to 572).[^4]

The full chapter is available in the corpus translation.[^1]

[^1]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, [ch. 2](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/02-i-influence-de-la-race-sur-la-liberte/translation/).
[^2]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, [ch. 2](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/02-i-influence-de-la-race-sur-la-liberte/translation/#p-27e58b).
[^3]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, [ch. 2](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/02-i-influence-de-la-race-sur-la-liberte/translation/#p-16f1c1).
[^4]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, [ch. 2](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/02-i-influence-de-la-race-sur-la-liberte/translation/#p-160bb0).
